frameworks.name: Boundary Architecture
frameworks.description: Boundaries are not walls — they are decision rules made in advance. Pre-decided rules eliminate real-time negotiation, which is where most boundaries collapse under social pressure.
steps: Identify your three most violated boundaries: where do you consistently agree to things you later resent?
For each, define the rule in advance: 'I do not take calls without a scheduled agenda.' Not 'I try to avoid...'
Define the enforcement mechanism: what do you actually say when the boundary is tested?
Distinguish boundary types: time (when you are available), energy (what you agree to do), information (what you share), relationship (who has access to you)
Audit your calendar and commitments: which existing commitments violate your own boundaries?
Design the exit protocol: how do you exit a commitment that is boundary-violating without burning the relationship?
antipatterns: Explaining boundaries rather than stating them — explanation invites negotiation
Emotional boundaries rather than behavioral rules — 'I need you to respect me' vs. 'I do not respond to messages after 8pm'
Inconsistent enforcement — intermittent reinforcement trains others to push harder, not less

frameworks.name: Demand Triage Protocol
frameworks.description: Not all demands on your time and attention are equal. Most feel urgent. Few are important. A triage system prevents the urgent from crowding out the important.
steps: Classify every incoming demand: Urgent+Important (do now), Important+Not Urgent (schedule), Urgent+Not Important (delegate or decline), Neither (eliminate)
Identify your demand sources: who or what generates the majority of your non-important urgent demands?
Create a default response template for each demand category — eliminates real-time decision cost
Establish response time norms explicitly: 'I respond to non-urgent messages within 48 hours' stated publicly reduces expectation pressure
Audit the 'yes' cost: every yes to a demand is a no to something else — make that tradeoff explicit before agreeing
Design your protected time first: block deep work and recovery before accepting any demand
antipatterns: Treating all demands as equal — email from a stranger and a message from your most important client are not the same
No default responses — deciding case-by-case burns decision capital and produces inconsistent enforcement
Protecting nothing — availability without limit produces output without direction

frameworks.name: High-Value Relationship Boundary Calibration
frameworks.description: Boundaries in high-stakes relationships (key clients, partners, family) require calibration — not elimination. The goal is sustainable access, not closed access.
steps: Define the relationship value: what does this relationship contribute to your 10-year trajectory?
Define your sustainable access level: how much access can you provide without resentment or depletion?
Communicate the boundary as a service standard, not a restriction: 'I give my best work when I have focused blocks — my standard is to respond within 24 hours'
Identify the real fear: most boundary failures in high-value relationships are fear of disappointing, losing, or being judged — name that fear explicitly
Test: enforce the boundary once, observe the response. Most relationships absorb reasonable boundaries without damage.
Exit criteria: at what point does this relationship cost more in boundary violations than it contributes in value?
antipatterns: All-or-nothing: the choice is not 'unlimited access' or 'end the relationship' — calibration is available
Assuming others know your limits — boundaries not communicated are not boundaries
Fear-based compliance disguised as generosity — saying yes when you mean no is not generosity

prompts.name: Boundary Audit
prompts.template: Audit my boundaries. The three commitments I most resent: [list]. The demands that most consistently invade my protected time: [list]. The relationships where I most frequently override my own limits: [list]. Identify my most violated boundary, the rule that would prevent the violation, and the enforcement script.

prompts.name: Demand Triage Design
prompts.template: Design my demand triage system. Primary demand sources: [list people/channels]. Current response time norms: [describe]. Protected time blocks I want to establish: [describe]. Give me a triage classification system, default response templates, and the explicit rules for my protected time.

prompts.name: Boundary Enforcement Script
prompts.template: Write a boundary enforcement script for: [specific situation — e.g., client who expects same-day responses, colleague who schedules meetings without asking, family member who expects unlimited availability]. Tone required: [warm/neutral/firm]. Relationship value: [describe]. Give me the actual words to say or write.

examples.title: Consultant Reclaiming Deep Work Time
examples.context: Consultant with 6 active clients, all expecting same-day responses, unable to produce high-quality work
examples.boundary_defined: No meetings before 1pm. Email responded to once daily at 4pm. Stated explicitly in onboarding documents.
examples.communication: 'My best work happens in focused morning blocks. I respond to all messages by 4pm ET — if something is genuinely urgent, call me.'
examples.outcome: Initial resistance from 1 of 6 clients (who churned). Remaining 5 accepted. Work quality improved measurably. Revenue per hour increased 35% due to recovered deep work capacity.

vocabulary.definition: A pre-decided rule governing what you will and will not agree to. Effective boundaries are behavioral and specific, not emotional and general.
Classifying incoming demands by urgency and importance to prevent urgent-but-unimportant demands from crowding out important-but-non-urgent work.
Pre-written language for communicating a boundary when tested. Eliminates real-time decision-making under social pressure.
The implicit cost of agreement: every yes is a no to something else. Making the tradeoff explicit before agreeing changes the decision.
The level of availability you can maintain without resentment or depletion — the boundary calibration target for high-value relationships.
Calendar blocks reserved for high-value, self-directed work before any external demands are accepted. The first entry in the calendar, not the last.

sources.title: The 4-Hour Workweek
sources.relevance: Demand triage, batching, protected time architecture

sources.title: Essentialism
sources.relevance: Demand prioritization, saying no, protecting high-value time

sources.title: Deep Work
sources.relevance: Protected deep work time, availability cost

master_laws: Law 29 (Energy — sustainable access requires energy management)
